author: @andrewknights6204
text:
Happy Birthday and thanks for the videos.  When I first wanted to build a railway in my garden, I needed to buy a MIG welder.  In thelocal shop I asked the man there how to tell if the weld was good. "Hit it with a big hammer" he said. "If it breaks you need to do a better job next time!"
Great fun and extremely useful, don't know how I did without it.  Only done various grades of steel but so useful, not just for garden trains! Best wishes for your vidoes.

author: @miketanner1920
text:
Just wait until you get your hands on a tig welder 
Welding is a journey into metal and your imagination 
Have fun just remember that safety is essential when dealing with excited electricity and metals and alloys 
One key thing that I had given to me was when ever you are welding a unfamiliar martial test your settings on scrap peace's of the same martial to insure the best welds on your finished project 
Never wear synthetic clothes or shoes when welding unless it is specifically designed for welding ok that's enough preaching 
God bless you and your's 
Have fun and be safe 
Thanks for the videos 
Just saying

author: @richardsandwell2285
text: A can of Anti-Spatter spray is most helpful, to prevent spatter sticking inside the nozzle shroud and side cutters to trim back the wire. You can use them outside if it is not too windy, I used to turn the gas pressure up a bit and or create wind shields. For Stainless and Aluminium use pure Argon gas, but for Steel a blend of Argon 95% and CO2 5% is good, if you are to buy one gas only then I would buy pure Argon, you can get away with using it on Steel. Aluminium Welding is a bit of a Black Art but it is like anything, practice makes perfect. It is easier to use than a stick welder, they earned the nick name EASY2 because in the early days they were often used on CO2 gas. And they are so easy a child could use one after an hours practice. The weld penetration is generally less than with a stick welder, but on stainless because of the low heat conductivity the welds are just has strong. The nice thing is being able to turn down the juice and blob together impossibly thin bits of junk and build up areas with layers of weld.  No chipping off slag between welds. Happy Birthday and Happy Welding.

author: @marcogallazzi9049
text: Happy Birthday Rob!! Very nice present you got there :). Btw, you can get yourself some flux core wire to practice, before you spend on a Mig gas cilinder (actually a 75/25% mix between argon and CO2). Flux core will splatter a lot but is good to learn Mig welding. There´s basically only two things to it, amps and wire speed, and that will depend on the thickness of your material, you just need to make some tests on spare material first before you actually weld. When using gas its a fixed pressure, so nothing to do there.

author: @twestgard2
text: The fear I have of welding is that I can’t figure out how it goes wrong, and because of that I don’t know what to avoid doing. I know that people sometimes accidentally kill themselves with a welder, but I don’t know what exactly leads up to that moment. So the only way I know to not do that is to not use an electric welder at all. I don’t understand why it’s so hard to get this explained but I’ve read several books about welding, asked several welders, and watched hundreds of YouTube videos and I can’t find an explanation anywhere. 🤷‍♂️

replies.author: @twestgard2
replies.text: @obsculor exactly, I can get a million explanations what to point toward, but for some reason, nobody wants to say what to avoid. It’s a really weird omission in my opinion.

replies.author: @bazofiascbr
replies.text:
@twestgard2 I think 2 things can go wrong: the electrode sticks, then you need to break it off or quickly turn off the welding machine - or - there's too much power and you melt what you meant to solder.
Don't touch the metal you are welding, don't weld on wet places - should be good tips to keep you safe. But I'm sure there's more, I'm no expert welder.

author: @Authoratah
text:
I've watched other so-called beginners videos on MIG welders before but I didn't learn anything...they assume the person watching knows all about the machine and the setup.
This was the most informative and to the point video I've seen ...for beginners.

replies.author: @twestgard2
replies.text: Yes, it’s a really weird gap in the YouTube instructional genre. It’s like if someone tried to teach you to drive but never mentioned that there’s a door you have to open and a keyhole that requires a key. It’s a factor in welding and also masonry for some reason. I think there are a ton of people who want to explain their newer skills and don’t actually give any thought to people who don’t know the things they learned earlier in the process.

author: @Buzzhumma
text: Before you try gas and getting the bottles I suggest you get some decent quality gasless mig wire . I have been using it for years and outside use most of the time . Welding gases are not cheap and mostly they are hired so you have to pay by time as well! Gasless can do stainless and mild steel .Just make sure its a reputable brand as the cheap stuff varies a lot in the flux consistency and is very frustrating!
